content:
- Marked the network-fault task done with all acceptance boxes checked and `<passes>true</passes>`.
- Ran `.ralph/task_switch.sh`, which refreshed current task summaries, appended task history, and sent the completion email.
- Next: stage the full worktree, commit with the required task-finished message including validation evidence and implementation notes, then push.

- Completed story-11 task 03: implemented typed external sink faults in the long-lane webhook chaos gateway and added the external network-fault e2e scenario with retry/resume, convergence, post-recovery update, and verify assertions.
- All required lanes passed: `make check`, `make lint`, `make test`, `make test-long`.
- Marked the task done, ran `.ralph/task_switch.sh`, committed/pushed as `dbf35b9` plus follow-up Ralph-summary cleanup `bbfc1e2`; worktree is clean and the branch is up to date.

- Read the Ralph progress log plus `.ralph/current_tasks.md` and `.ralph/current_tasks_done.md`.
- Confirmed the active backlog is currently stories 11 through 15 and there are no active bug tasks shown in the summaries.
- Next I am reading the underlying task files to resolve `blocked_by`, `priority`, and story-chain ordering before writing `.ralph/current_task.txt`.

- Read the active task files directly.
- Verified the active backlog entries currently visible in stories 11-15 do not declare explicit `<priority>` or `<blocked_by>` tags in their task bodies.
- Next I am confirming the repository shape and current implementation stream so I can choose the most logical failing task, which currently points to continuing story 11 rather than jumping ahead.

- Confirmed the repository is a Rust workspace (`Cargo.toml`, `crates/runner`, `crates/source-bootstrap`, `crates/ingest-contract`) with end-to-end test harnesses already concentrated in `crates/runner/tests`.
- Chose to continue story 11 because the active failing backlog has no visible bug tasks, no visible blocker tags, no explicit higher priorities, and the most coherent next step is the next chaos E2E task immediately after the completed story-11 task 03.
- Next I will write `.ralph/current_task.txt` to point at story-11 task 04 and stop.

- Wrote `.ralph/current_task.txt` with `.ralph/tasks/story-11-e2e-chaos/04-task-e2e-transaction-failure-recovery.md`.
- Verified the active failing backlog still has no visible unblocked bug tasks ahead of it, and continuing story 11 is the most coherent next step after completing task 03.
- If this context ends now, the next agent should open story-11 task 04, use the `tdd` skill as required by the task body, implement it, and then validate `make check`, `make test`, and `make lint` plus `make test-long` if impacted.

- Read previous Ralph progress entries, `.ralph/current_task.txt`, and the required `tdd` plus `improve-code-boundaries` skills.
- Confirmed the active task path is `.ralph/tasks/story-11-e2e-chaos/04-task-e2e-transaction-failure-recovery.md`.
- Next I am reading the task body to determine whether this turn must create/adjust a plan or execute an existing `NOW EXECUTE` plan.

- Read the story-11 task 04 body. It is still `not_started` with no `NOW EXECUTE` marker, so this turn must create and finalize a plan rather than implementing.
- Confirmed there is not yet a `_plans` directory for task 04; earlier story-11 tasks each have one dated plan file.
- Next I am reading prior plan files to mirror the expected structure before writing the new transaction-failure recovery plan.

- Read prior story-11 plan files. They end with a detailed execution plan and an explicit `NOW EXECUTE` marker once the design is considered ready.
- Next I am inspecting the current long-lane tests and support/runtime files around reconcile, persistence, and any existing transaction-failure hooks so the new plan reflects real boundaries rather than guesswork.

- Inspected the current long-lane and harness support. Story-11 tasks 01-03 already added typed boundaries for external sink faults, runner lifecycle, destination locks, and tracking progress snapshots.
- The new task should build on those existing test-support boundaries rather than inventing product hooks.
- Next I am reading the real destination transaction code in webhook persistence, reconcile, and tracking-state updates to choose an honest way to force transaction failures on the destination side.

- Confirmed task files record the chosen plan with a trailing `<plan>...</plan>` tag.
- The likely honest failure mechanism is destination-side SQL behavior, not product failpoints, but I still need to verify the runner's ingest behavior before locking that into the plan.
- Next I am reading the webhook ingest path to understand whether helper-persistence transaction failures should be modeled as caller-visible retries, runner process failure, or both.

- Wrote the task-04 execution plan at `.ralph/tasks/story-11-e2e-chaos/04-task-e2e-transaction-failure-recovery_plans/2026-04-19-transaction-failure-recovery-plan.md` and added its path to the task markdown.
- The plan is marked `NOW EXECUTE`; it uses `tdd` slices and an `improve-code-boundaries` boundary cleanup around destination-side failure support, gateway observation, and runner-exit handling.
- Next turn should read the plan, then execute it as written: add the transaction-failure long-lane scenarios, implement the minimum support boundaries, and drive all required lanes (`make check`, `make lint`, `make test`, `make test-long`) to green.

- Read prior progress, confirmed the active task is story-11 task 04, and re-read the required `tdd` plus `improve-code-boundaries` skill instructions.
- Confirmed previous context already created a `NOW EXECUTE` plan for this task, so this turn should execute that plan rather than re-planning.
- Next I am reading the task markdown and plan path, then I will inspect the relevant runtime and long-lane harness files before starting the first RED test slice.

- Read the task markdown and the existing execution plan. The plan ends with `NOW EXECUTE`, so the task is in implementation mode.
- Confirmed the worktree is intentionally dirty from Ralph progress/task files and the new task plan directory; I will avoid reverting any of that.
- Next I am reading the long-lane test/support files and relevant contract tests to start with the first RED slice for helper-persistence transaction failure.

- Added the first implementation slice: a new `destination_write_failure` support module, runner failed-exit support, gateway forwarded-status sequence support, renamed the gateway harness constructor to an observation-focused name, and added the first ignored helper-persistence long-lane test.
- The new helper-failure test currently covers only the tracer-bullet behavior: destination-side helper write failure causes retries and eventual convergence after the failure is removed.
- Next I am running just that ignored long-lane test to get the actual RED/GREEN signal and tighten the implementation around any compile or runtime failures it exposes.

- The helper-persistence long-lane scenario is now green with stronger assertions: helper shadow and destination stay at the old value while the helper-table trigger is active, the gateway observes downstream `500 -> 200`, and `runner verify` still reports only the real migrated table.
- The reconcile-failure long-lane scenario is also green in isolation: helper state advances, destination stays pinned, reconcile failure is durably recorded, the runner exits, restart recovers, and `runner verify` still passes.
- Next I am formatting the code and then running the full required lanes: `make check`, `make lint`, `make test`, and `make test-long`.

- Completed story-11 task 04 implementation. Added two real long-lane transaction-failure recovery scenarios plus dedicated destination write-failure support, generalized gateway downstream-status observation, and explicit expected runner-failure waiting.
- All required lanes passed: `make check`, `make lint`, `make test`, `make test-long`.
- Next I am running `.ralph/task_switch.sh`, then I will review the resulting worktree, commit everything with the required task-finished message, and push.
